children.name: Acanthiza
children.description: The thornbills — a genus of small, active insectivores with fine, pointed bills and a habit of flicking their wings while foraging. They are the most species-rich genus in the family, occupying virtually every terrestrial habitat across Australasia. Their calls are typically high-pitched and insect-like, and their social structures range from monogamous pairs to complex cooperative groups.

children.name: Acanthiza chrysorrhoa
children.description: A small, active thornbill with a striking yellow rump, a white eyebrow, and finely streaked underparts, widespread across Australia. It is one of the most conspicuous thornbills, often seen in open woodlands and grasslands, where it forages on the ground and in low vegetation. Yellow-rumped Thornbills are highly social, foraging in flocks and often associating with other insectivorous birds.

children.name: Acanthiza pusilla
children.description: A small, nondescript brown thornbill with a rufous forehead, a finely streaked breast, and a thin, high-pitched call, common in the forests and woodlands of eastern and southeastern Australia. It is an active, acrobatic forager, constantly gleaning insects from foliage and bark in the understorey. Its simple but musical song is a series of high-pitched notes and trills.

children.name: Acanthiza lineata
children.description: A tiny, olive-green thornbill with a finely streaked crown and a distinctive brown ear-patch, found in eucalypt forests of eastern and southern Australia. It forages primarily in the canopy, gleaning insects from leaves and bark in fast-moving, restless flocks. Its call is a high-pitched, metallic twittering, and it is a core member of mixed-species feeding flocks.

children.name: Sericornis
children.description: The scrubwrens — a genus of secretive, primarily ground-dwelling warblers with a furtive, mouse-like behaviour, found in the densest parts of forests and heathlands. They have short, rounded wings and strong legs, and their plumage is typically a rich blend of browns, olive-greens, and rufous. Most species are restricted to Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Sericornis frontalis
children.description: A small, skulking bird with a distinctive white eyebrow, a dark ear-patch, and a rich, bubbling song that emerges from the densest thickets, common throughout coastal eastern and southern Australia. It forages on or near the ground, methodically turning over leaves in search of invertebrates.

children.name: Sericornis humilis
children.description: A dull olive-brown scrubwren with a pale throat and eyebrow, endemic to Tasmania and the Bass Strait islands. It inhabits dense, moist understorey in rainforests, wet eucalypt forests, and temperate heathlands. It is more secretive than its mainland relatives, keeping to the darkest, dampest thickets.

children.name: Smicrornis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Weebill, which at 8–9 cm is one of the smallest birds in Australia. It is a compact, active insectivore with a short tail, a pale, slightly curved bill, and a persistent, loud call that seems far too big for such a tiny bird. It is common across much of the continent in dry forests and woodlands.

children.name: Smicrornis brevirostris
children.description: Australia's smallest bird, a tiny olive-grey warbler with a short, stubby bill and a remarkably loud, piping call that carries long distances through the eucalypt canopy. It feeds exclusively on insects and spiders, gleaning them from the outer foliage of trees in the mid-to-upper canopy. It is a common but often overlooked resident of dry woodlands across mainland Australia.
